trace.rationale: Historical data shows that in MLB games, the probability of at least one run being scored in the first inning is around 55-60%, based on general patterns from pre-cutoff seasons. No specific team or pitcher adjustments are provided, so the baseline estimate is used.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 48-52% of first innings have at least one run scored, with slight variation by team and pitcher. No specific game context provided, so baseline probability is used.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of games have a run scored in the first inning, with no strong evidence to deviate for this specific matchup.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55-60% of first innings have at least one run scored, with slight variation by team and pitcher. No specific game context provided, so baseline estimate.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 50-55% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific evidence to deviate from this baseline.

Historical data shows that in MLB games, a run is scored in the first inning roughly 45-50% of the time, but recent trends and team-specific factors (e.g., Team A's strong offense and Team B's average pitching) slightly increase the probability. The cutoff is near the game time, so no weather or postponement issues are expected.
